passages.explanation: Basil arrives as the voice of moral conscience, horrified and grief-stricken, only to find Dorian sipping wine and looking 'dreadfully bored.' The gulf between Basil's anguish and Dorian's indifference measures exactly how far Dorian has fallen. His parroting of Lord Henry's philosophy, 'If one doesn't talk about a thing, it has never happened,' reveals how completely he has internalized the idea that language and expression create reality, not the other way around.

Basil's outrage at Dorian attending the opera while Sibyl lay dead forces a confrontation between genuine moral feeling and the aesthete's cultivated detachment. Dorian's response, that 'only shallow people require years to get rid of an emotion,' is Henry's philosophy weaponized. Basil correctly identifies that something fundamental has changed in Dorian, and he names the source: Harry's influence. But it is also Dorian's own nature asserting itself.

The exchange where Dorian says 'you only taught me to be vain' wounds Basil deeply, and Wilde reveals the painful truth that both of Dorian's mentors have contributed to his corruption: Basil by worshipping his beauty, Henry by teaching him to worship it himself. Dorian's long, eloquent speech reframing Sibyl's death as romantic martyrdom shows his terrifying facility for turning human suffering into narrative. He has become a connoisseur of his own experience.

Basil's reluctant surrender, choosing to preserve the friendship rather than press the moral point, shows how love can become complicity. The shift to the portrait crisis, when Basil wants to see his painting and Dorian recoils in terror, introduces the chapter's real stakes. The portrait has become Dorian's secret self, and any exposure threatens the entire architecture of his double life.

Dorian's extreme reaction to Basil approaching the screen, threatening to end their friendship forever, reveals the intensity of his need to keep his corruption hidden. His terror is not just practical but existential: if Basil sees the changed portrait, the myth of Dorian's innocence collapses. Wilde dramatizes how the maintenance of a beautiful public self requires increasingly desperate acts of concealment.

The revelation that Basil planned to exhibit the portrait in Paris raises the stakes enormously. But it also triggers a pivotal deflection: Dorian turns the conversation to Basil's secret, remembering Henry's hint. Basil's long confession of his devotion to Dorian, his account of worshipping him and putting too much of himself into the painting, is one of the novel's most vulnerable moments. Wilde explores how the artist's obsessive love for beauty mirrors and enables the subject's narcissism.

Dorian's relief that Basil's secret is merely adoration, not knowledge of the portrait's change, is deeply ironic. He has just witnessed a genuine confession of love and vulnerability, and his primary response is strategic: he is safe. Wilde shows how narcissism makes empathy impossible. Dorian can observe Basil's pain with intellectual interest but cannot truly feel it, having already crossed into the territory where other people's emotions are spectacles rather than calls to connection.

The closing dialogue is rich with dramatic irony. Basil's confession that 'something seems to have gone out of me' after speaking his devotion aloud echoes the novel's theme that expression can be a form of loss. Dorian's casual dismissal of the confession as 'not even a compliment' is cruel in its obliviousness. When Dorian says he would go to Basil rather than Harry in trouble, it rings hollow: he has already chosen Henry's worldview completely.

The final paragraphs show Dorian smiling to himself after Basil leaves, savoring the irony of having extracted a secret while protecting his own. His reflection that Basil's friendship was 'coloured by romance' reduces genuine devotion to an aesthetic category. The closing line, about hiding the portrait 'at all costs,' signals the transition from moral crisis to practical management. Dorian's corruption is now something to be organized, not confronted.
